title: Fancy X Desk
summary: A desk on two stacked-2x4 "I"-shaped legs joined by an X-braced cross member — the stacking pattern is what makes plain construction lumber look intentional.
description:
The legs are the whole design idea: rather than a single 4x4 post, each leg is built as an "I" shape from layered 2x4s, and it's the repetition of that stacked pattern that gives the desk its presence — the source is right that "it's the stacking that makes these legs so beautiful." None of the individual joints are complicated; the visual payoff comes purely from doing the same simple lamination twice, identically.

The X brace between the two leg towers is the one place with real angle-cutting — mitered at 45° so the two diagonal pieces cross evenly in the middle. Build the two leg towers as identical, separate assemblies first and confirm they match before adding the brace or the top, the same principle behind any piece with mirrored end assemblies.

materials.name: Pine, 2x12, 6 ft
materials.note: 4 - 2x6 @ 6 ft also work as a substitute for the top.

cutList.note: Long point to long point, both ends cut not parallel at 45° off square.
Long point to long point, both ends cut not parallel at 45° off square.
Long point to short point, both ends cut parallel at 45° off square — cut to fit.

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body: From 2x4 stock (actual 1 1/2" x 3 1/2") cut two leg tower center posts at 20", four leg tower stretchers at 21", three cross stretchers at 59", eight X-brace diagonals at 10 3/4" and four longer X-brace diagonals at 21 3/4" — all the X pieces measured long point to long point with both ends cut at 45 degrees off square and NOT parallel to each other — plus two cross braces at 30 3/4" with both 45 degree ends parallel. Those parts come to 535 1/2" of 2x4, which needs six 8 ft boards with a 1/8" kerf; the materials list only calls for five, so buy a sixth before you start. From the 8 ft 1x4 cut four leg tower end caps at 18" and four leg tower caps at 3 1/2" — one board covers all eight. Leave the two 2x12s at their full 72" factory length for the tabletop; a 6 ft board is exactly 72", so there is nothing to trim off. Cut the 45 degree pieces last, after the towers are standing, so you can fit them to the opening you actually have.

steps.title: Build the two leg towers
steps.body: Each leg tower is an "I": the 20" 2x4 center post with an 18" 1x4 end cap fastened across each end so the cap projects past the post on both sides. Build both towers at the same time on a flat surface, laying the second one out directly against the first and comparing them part for part — everything downstream bolts to both towers at once, so a difference between them cannot be corrected later. Glue and 2 1/2" self-tapping screws through the caps into the post ends, countersunk so the heads sit flush and disappear under finish. Check that each tower is square across the cap and that the two towers are the same overall height before moving on.

steps.title: Add the bracing
steps.body: Add the 21" leg tower stretchers to each tower, two per tower, keeping them square to the post and identically placed on both towers — measure each stretcher's position from the same end of the post on each tower rather than eyeballing the pair. Predrill every hole with a countersink bit before driving 2 1/2" screws; 2x4 pine splits along the grain if you drive a screw near an end dry, and a split shows through paint. Glue each joint as well as screwing it. Set the two towers side by side again afterward and confirm the stretchers still line up across the pair.

steps.title: Attach the top and bottom caps
steps.body: Fit the four 3 1/2" leg tower caps — one at the top and one at the bottom of each tower — trimming them on the miter saw to sit flush with the surfaces they land on. These are short pieces, so cut them with a stop block or clamp a scrap fence to the saw rather than freehanding four little cuts; a 3 1/2" offcut is also too short to hold safely by hand near the blade. Glue and screw each cap, countersinking as before, and sight down the tower afterward to confirm the caps did not push anything out of line.

steps.title: Stack the 2x4 layers
steps.body: Build up the remaining 2x4 layers on each tower, gluing and screwing each layer to the one below it — the repetition of that stack is the entire design idea, so keep the layers flush at the faces where they show and let any discrepancy fall on the hidden inside face. Work layer for layer on both towers together rather than finishing one tower and then copying it: comparing after every layer catches a 1/16" drift while it is still one screw away from being fixed. Countersink every screw and stagger the screw positions between layers so you are not driving into the last layer's screws. Set the two towers face to face when you are done; they should match everywhere.

steps.title: Join the towers with cross stretchers
steps.body: Stand the two towers upright, parallel and the same way round, and connect them with the three 59" cross stretchers — that length sets the distance between the towers and, with the 72" top, leaves the top overhanging past each tower. Fasten with 2 1/2" pocket screws or countersunk 2 1/2" screws and glue, and clamp everything before driving anything. Measure both diagonals across the assembled base and adjust until they match; from here on the base is what the X brace has to fit into, so a base that is out of square makes every 45 degree cut wrong. Check that all four feet touch the floor at once on a flat surface.

steps.title: Add the X cross bracing
steps.body: Cut the X pieces to the opening you actually have rather than to the list — the two 30 3/4" cross braces carry a 'cut to fit' note and the same applies to the eight 10 3/4" and four 21 3/4" diagonals, whose lengths are long point to long point. Set the miter saw to 45 degrees and cut a test piece from an offcut first, holding it in the opening to confirm the angle points the right way before you commit good stock; on the diagonals the two ends are cut at 45 degrees but NOT parallel to each other, which is easy to get backwards. Work from the center outward so the two arms of the X meet cleanly on the centerline, and dry-fit the whole X before any glue. Fasten with glue and countersunk 2 1/2" screws driven at an angle through the brace ends into the stretchers.

steps.title: Attach the top and finish
steps.body: Join the two 72" 2x12s edge to edge into one top 22 1/2" wide, using 2 1/2" pocket screws and glue along the seam with the boards clamped flat on a level surface — build the top as its own assembly and let the glue cure before it goes anywhere near the base. Alternate the boards' crown so the seam stays flat, and keep the pocket holes on the underside. Then set the top on the base, center it so the overhang is equal at each end, and fasten up through the top stretchers into the underside of the top; a sheet of plywood makes a smoother writing surface if you would rather not deal with a glue seam. Sand every surface up through the grits, ease all four top edges, and apply your finish.

images.alt: Fancy X desk with stacked 2x4 leg towers
